teamObjective.explanation: I hold only low spade singles (3-6) with no trump cards. On the opening lead, the best I can do is play my weakest card to minimize value loss and give my partner a chance to win the trick, transferring the lead to our team if possible.

partnerBelief.summary: Partner (player 2) has 3 cards and, because my own hand is very weak, is more likely than baseline to hold higher cards or trumps that can beat a low single. No specific distribution can be inferred from public events.

opponentBelief.summary: Opponents (players 1 and 3) each hold 3 cards. With no cards played yet, they are likely to hold cards higher than 3 of spades and can probably beat this lead, but this remains probabilistic.

actionRationale.primaryReason: Leading the 3 of spades discards the weakest card while preserving the 4, 5, and 6 for future tricks. Any of these low singles is unlikely to win, so minimizing the value of the card spent is optimal. It also creates a low-stakes trick where partner may be able to take control cheaply.

whyNotAlternatives.reason: The 4 is still a very low card and almost as likely to lose as the 3, but it is strictly more valuable; leading the 3 first preserves it.
The 5 is the second-strongest card in my hand and should be saved for a future trick rather than spent on a lead that is unlikely to win.
The 6 is my highest card and the only one with a slightly better chance of winning, but it is still a non-trump low card that will likely be beaten. Saving it maximizes my remaining hand strength.

risks: Any opponent likely has a card higher than 3 of spades and will win the trick.
Partner may not be able to beat opponent responses, ceding lead to the opposing team.
A lost lead could let opponents return with a high card or bomb that my weak hand cannot answer.
riskAssessment.mitigation: By leading the lowest card, I limit the value surrendered and keep my slightly stronger cards (4-6) for later plays, maximizing whatever flexibility a weak hand can offer.

notes: Opening lead with no public information beyond starting hand sizes; all hidden-card beliefs remain probabilistic.
